quiz_questions.question_text: What is the defining property of an orthogonal matrix Q?

options.text: All entries of Q are positive

quiz_questions.explanation: An orthogonal matrix satisfies Q^T Q = I, meaning its transpose equals its inverse. This is equivalent to saying its columns form an orthonormal set.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the determinant of any orthogonal matrix?
quiz_questions.explanation: Since Q^T Q = I, det(Q^T)det(Q) = 1, so (det Q)^2 = 1, giving det(Q) = +1 or -1.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following 2x2 matrices is orthogonal? (c = cos(theta), s = sin(theta))
quiz_questions.explanation: The 2x2 rotation matrix [[cos(theta), -sin(theta)], [sin(theta), cos(theta)]] is orthogonal because its columns are orthonormal: column dot products give cos^2 + sin^2 = 1 and -sc + sc = 0.

quiz_questions.question_text: If Q is an orthogonal matrix and x is any vector, what is ||Qx|| compared to ||x||?
quiz_questions.explanation: Orthogonal matrices preserve vector norms: ||Qx||^2 = (Qx)^T(Qx) = x^T Q^T Q x = x^T I x = ||x||^2. This means orthogonal transformations are length-preserving (isometries).

quiz_questions.question_text: The identity matrix I is an orthogonal matrix. What is I^T?
quiz_questions.explanation: The identity matrix is symmetric, so I^T = I. It trivially satisfies I^T I = I*I = I, confirming it is orthogonal.

quiz_questions.question_text: If P and Q are both orthogonal matrices of the same size, is PQ also orthogonal?
quiz_questions.explanation: The product of orthogonal matrices is orthogonal: (PQ)^T(PQ) = Q^T P^T P Q = Q^T I Q = Q^T Q = I.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a 2x2 orthogonal matrix with determinant -1?
quiz_questions.explanation: [[1,0],[0,-1]] has orthonormal columns (1,0) and (0,-1), and det = -1. This is a reflection matrix (reflecting across the x-axis).

quiz_questions.question_text: What are the possible eigenvalues of a real orthogonal matrix?

options.text: +1, -1, or complex numbers with absolute value 1

quiz_questions.explanation: Since orthogonal matrices preserve norms, all eigenvalues must have absolute value 1. For real orthogonal matrices, real eigenvalues are +1 or -1, and complex eigenvalues come in conjugate pairs on the unit circle.

quiz_questions.question_text: If Q is a 3x3 orthogonal matrix with det(Q) = 1, what type of transformation does Q represent?
quiz_questions.explanation: An orthogonal matrix with determinant +1 is called a proper orthogonal matrix (or special orthogonal), and it represents a rotation. Determinant -1 indicates an improper rotation (rotation composed with a reflection).

quiz_questions.question_text: If Q is orthogonal, what is Q^T Q + Q Q^T for a square orthogonal matrix?
quiz_questions.explanation: For a square orthogonal matrix, both Q^T Q = I and Q Q^T = I (since Q^(-1) = Q^T works on both sides). Therefore Q^T Q + Q Q^T = I + I = 2I.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'orthogonal transformation' mean geometrically?

options.text: A rigid transformation that preserves distances and angles between vectors
A transformation that projects vectors onto a line
A transformation that maps all vectors to the origin

quiz_questions.explanation: An orthogonal transformation is a rigid motion (isometry) that preserves lengths, angles, and the overall geometry of the space. It includes rotations and reflections.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'rotation matrix'?

options.text: Any matrix with all positive entries
An orthogonal matrix with determinant +1 that rotates vectors by a fixed angle
A diagonal matrix with entries between 0 and 1
A matrix whose eigenvalues are all real

quiz_questions.explanation: A rotation matrix is a special orthogonal matrix (det = +1) that rotates all vectors by a fixed angle while preserving lengths and orientation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'reflection matrix'?

options.text: An orthogonal matrix with determinant -1 that reflects vectors across a hyperplane
A matrix equal to its own transpose
A matrix with all negative entries
The inverse of any rotation matrix

quiz_questions.explanation: A reflection matrix is an orthogonal matrix with determinant -1. It reflects vectors across a hyperplane (line in 2D, plane in 3D), reversing orientation while preserving distances.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'isometry' mean in the context of orthogonal matrices?

options.text: A mapping that doubles all distances
A mapping that preserves only angles, not distances
A mapping that sends all vectors to unit vectors

quiz_questions.explanation: An isometry is a transformation that preserves the distance between any two points. Orthogonal matrices represent linear isometries because ||Qx - Qy|| = ||Q(x-y)|| = ||x-y||.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'special orthogonal group' SO(n)?

options.text: The set of all n x n matrices
The set of all n x n orthogonal matrices with determinant +1
The set of all n x n diagonal matrices
The set of all n x n symmetric matrices

quiz_questions.explanation: SO(n) is the special orthogonal group consisting of all n x n orthogonal matrices with determinant +1. These represent pure rotations in n-dimensional space and form a group under matrix multiplication.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'orthogonal group O(n)' refer to?

options.text: The set of all n x n orthogonal matrices (det = +1 or -1)
The set of all n-dimensional vectors
The set of n x n matrices with orthogonal rows only
The set of all invertible n x n matrices

quiz_questions.explanation: O(n) is the orthogonal group of all n x n real matrices Q satisfying Q^T Q = I. It includes both rotations (det = +1) and improper rotations/reflections (det = -1).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'Givens rotation'?

options.text: A rotation in a 2D plane embedded in a higher-dimensional space, used to zero out specific matrix entries
A rotation that maps every vector to its negative
The rotation associated with the largest eigenvalue

quiz_questions.explanation: A Givens rotation is an orthogonal matrix that performs rotation in a single coordinate plane (i,j), modifying only two rows/columns. It is used in QR algorithms to zero out individual matrix entries.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'orthogonal similarity' mean for matrices A and B?

options.text: A and B have the same determinant
There exists an orthogonal matrix Q such that B = Q^T A Q
A and B are both orthogonal

quiz_questions.explanation: Two matrices are orthogonally similar if B = Q^T A Q for some orthogonal Q. This is a special case of matrix similarity where the change-of-basis matrix is orthogonal.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'Householder matrix'?

options.text: A diagonal matrix with entries +1 or -1
An orthogonal reflection matrix of the form H = I - 2vv^T where v is a unit vector
A rotation matrix with angle pi/4
The matrix Q in a QR factorization

quiz_questions.explanation: A Householder matrix H = I - 2vv^T (v unit) is an orthogonal, symmetric matrix that reflects vectors across the hyperplane perpendicular to v. It has det = -1 and is its own inverse.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'unitary matrix' mean, and how does it relate to orthogonal matrices?

options.text: A unitary matrix is the complex generalization of an orthogonal matrix, satisfying U*U = I where U* is the conjugate transpose
A unitary matrix has all entries equal to 1
A unitary matrix is any matrix with unit determinant
A unitary matrix is the same as an orthogonal matrix

quiz_questions.explanation: A unitary matrix satisfies U*U = I (where U* = conjugate transpose). When restricted to real entries, the conjugate transpose becomes the regular transpose, making unitary matrices the complex extension of orthogonal matrices.

quiz_questions.question_text: A computer graphics engine uses the rotation matrix Q = [[cos(30), -sin(30)], [sin(30), cos(30)]] to rotate a sprite. If the sprite's position vector is v = (4, 0), what is the y-component of the rotated position? (Use cos(30) = sqrt(3)/2, sin(30) = 1/2.)

A robotics engineer models a robotic arm joint using a 3x3 rotation matrix. She composes two rotations R1 and R2. If R1 rotates by 45 degrees and R2 rotates by 30 degrees (both about the same axis), what is the total rotation angle of R2*R1?

options.text: The product is not a rotation

quiz_questions.explanation: Rotations about the same axis compose by adding angles. R2*R1 rotates by 45 + 30 = 75 degrees. The product of two rotation matrices is also a rotation matrix.

quiz_questions.question_text: A signal processing engineer applies an orthogonal matrix Q to a signal vector x with ||x|| = 10. After the transformation, she measures ||Qx||. What value does she get?
quiz_questions.explanation: Orthogonal matrices preserve norms, so ||Qx|| = ||x|| = 10. This is because Q^T Q = I implies (Qx)^T(Qx) = x^T x.

quiz_questions.question_text: A structural engineer uses the Householder reflection H = I - 2vv^T with unit vector v = (1/sqrt(2), 1/sqrt(2)) to transform a force vector. What is H * (1, 0)?
quiz_questions.explanation: vv^T = [[1/2, 1/2],[1/2, 1/2]]. H = I - 2vv^T = [[0, -1],[-1, 0]]. H*(1,0) = (0, -1). This reflects across the line perpendicular to v, which is the line y = -x.

quiz_questions.question_text: A data scientist needs to solve a least-squares problem using QR factorization. If A = QR where Q is 5x3 orthogonal and R is 3x3 upper triangular, how does she solve Ax = b?
quiz_questions.explanation: From A = QR, the least-squares solution satisfies Rx = Q^T b. Since R is square and invertible, x = R^(-1) Q^T b. Using Q^T is efficient since Q has orthonormal columns (Q^T Q = I).

quiz_questions.question_text: A physicist applies a 3D rotation to transform coordinates in a crystal lattice. The rotation matrix R has eigenvalue 1 with eigenvector (1, 1, 1)/sqrt(3). What does this eigenvector represent physically?

options.text: The axis of rotation (the direction that remains unchanged)
The normal to the rotation plane

quiz_questions.explanation: The eigenvector with eigenvalue 1 corresponds to the rotation axis -- the direction that is unchanged by the rotation. For a 3D rotation, this is the axis about which all other vectors rotate.

quiz_questions.question_text: A satellite navigation system uses Givens rotations to update a QR factorization in real-time. If applying a Givens rotation to zero out one entry of a matrix, how many rows are modified?
quiz_questions.explanation: A Givens rotation operates in a single 2D plane, so it only modifies 2 rows (or columns) of the matrix. This makes it efficient for sparse updates in real-time applications.

quiz_questions.question_text: A machine learning engineer uses an orthogonal matrix to whiten (decorrelate) features. If the covariance matrix of the original data is C, what is the covariance after applying orthogonal transformation Q?
quiz_questions.explanation: When data X is transformed to QX, the new covariance is Q^T C Q. If Q is chosen as the eigenvector matrix of C, this diagonalizes C, decorrelating the features.

quiz_questions.question_text: A quantum computing researcher needs a unitary gate that maps |0> = (1,0) to (1/sqrt(2), 1/sqrt(2)) and |1> = (0,1) to (1/sqrt(2), -1/sqrt(2)). What is this gate called?
quiz_questions.explanation: The Hadamard gate H = (1/sqrt(2))[[1,1],[1,-1]] is a fundamental quantum gate. It is both unitary and Hermitian (H = H*), and creates superposition states from basis states.

quiz_questions.question_text: An aerospace engineer models satellite orientation using a 3x3 rotation matrix. After composing n rotations R1, R2, ..., Rn, she worries about numerical drift. What property should she check to verify the product is still (approximately) a rotation matrix?

options.text: All entries should be between 0 and 1
R^T R should be close to I and det(R) close to +1

quiz_questions.explanation: A valid rotation matrix must satisfy R^T R = I (orthogonality) and det(R) = +1 (proper rotation). Checking these conditions detects numerical drift from accumulated floating-point errors in composed rotations.
